lesson.menu_desc: Opinions in Everyday Life — I prefer / I'd rather — 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 12 — Expressing Preferences and Making Choices: Sharing What You Like and Why
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Vanessa Giuriati | Aula 12 — Preferences

listenings.text: People ask me all the time how I make decisions, so let me share how I think about choices. When two options are on the table, I rarely pick the obvious one right away. I prefer to slow down and weigh both sides. Honestly, I would rather take a little longer and feel sure than choose quickly and regret it. Take a simple example: working from home or going to the office. I enjoy the quiet of home more than the noise of a busy floor, so most days I choose to stay in. That said, I am not really into spending every single day alone, so once or twice a week I go in for the energy of the team. The trick is knowing what matters to you. For me, focus is worth more than convenience. So instead of asking which option is best in general, I ask which option is best for me, right now. That small change makes every choice feel lighter.

Here is something I wish more people understood about preferences. A preference is not a fact, and it is not a competition. When you say you would rather have tea than coffee, you are not saying coffee is bad. You are simply sharing what works for you. In English, this matters, because the way you express a choice can sound either confident or rude. If you say a flat no, the door closes. But if you say you are not really into something, or that you would rather do something else, the door stays open. You are honest, but you are also kind. Strong speakers know this. They do not pretend to like everything, and they do not attack what they dislike. They simply explain their choice and their reason. I prefer this, because it suits me better. That single sentence is polite, clear, and impossible to argue with. So next time someone offers you a choice, do not just pick. Tell them what you prefer, and tell them why. That is how a real conversation grows.

extra_audio.text: First, someone gives me two options. Then, instead of choosing right away, I weigh both sides. After that, I decide what matters most to me. Honestly, I would rather take my time than regret a quick choice. In the end, I explain what I prefer and why.
